subtopics: Put-Call Parity $\cdot$ Binomial Trees $\cdot$ Delta Hedging $\cdot$ Black-Scholes $\cdot$ Greeks $\cdot$ Volatility Smile
prerequisites: Topic 4 — Gaussian Distributions; Topic 23 — Nash Equilibrium (no-arbitrage logic)
learningObjectives: **State and apply Put-Call Parity** to price puts from calls and construct explicit arbitrage portfolios when parity is violated.
**Price options using one-step and two-step binomial trees** via risk-neutral pricing and replicating portfolios.
**Price American puts** and determine when early exercise is optimal.
**Explain Delta hedging** and the role of Gamma in dynamic rebalancing.
**State the Black-Scholes assumptions and PDE** and give the sign and intuition for each Greek.
**Explain the volatility smile** and its implications for the Black-Scholes model.

blocks.title: Key Relationships
blocks.content:
**Put-Call Parity (European options):** \[C - P = S - Ke^{-rT}\]

**Risk-neutral pricing (binomial tree):** \[p^{*} = \frac{e^{rT}- d}{u - d}\qquad V_{0} = e^{-rT}\bigl[p^{*}\,V_{u} + (1-p^{*})\,V_{d}\bigr]\]

**Replicating portfolio:** $\Delta = (V_{u}-V_{d})/(S_{u}-S_{d})$ (shares),\; $B = e^{-rT}(V_{d}\,S_{u}-V_{u}\,S_{d})/(S_{u}-S_{d})$ (bond).

blocks.solution:
**(a) Put-Call Parity.** For European options on a non-dividend-paying stock: \[C - P = S - Ke^{-rT}\] This follows from no-arbitrage: both sides replicate a forward contract with delivery price $K$. **(b) Fair put price.** \[P = C - S + Ke^{-rT}= 8 - 100 + 105e^{-0.05}= 8 - 100 + 99.88 = \boxed{\$7.88}\] **(c) Arbitrage when $P=\$10$.** The put is overpriced ($10 > 7.88$). Sell the overpriced put and hedge:
ActionToday$S_{T} > K$$S_{T} \leq K$
Sell put$+10$$0$$-(K-S_{T})$
Buy call$-8$$+(S_{T}-K)$$0$
Short stock$+100$$-S_{T}$$-S_{T}$
Lend $Ke^{-rT}=99.88$$-99.88$$+K$$+K$
Net$\mathbf{+2.12}$$0$$0$
Risk-free profit today: $10 - 8 + 100 - 99.88 = \boxed{\$2.12}$, with zero payoff at expiry in both scenarios. **Key takeaway:** Put-Call Parity: $C-P=S-Ke^{-rT}$. If violated, sell the overpriced side and buy the underpriced side plus a replicating hedge. Profit is locked in today.

**(a) Risk-neutral probability.**

With discrete discounting, $(1+r)=1.05$. Set $(1+r)S_{0} = p^{*} S_{u} + (1-p^{*})S_{d}$: \[1.05\times50 = 65p^{*} + 40(1-p^{*}) \implies 52.5 = 40 + 25p^{*} \implies p^{*} = \frac{12.5}{25}= \boxed{0.5}\]

**(b) Call price.** Payoffs: $C_{u} = \max(65-55,0)=10$,\; $C_{d} = \max(40-55,0)=0$. \[C_{0} = \frac{1}{1.05}\bigl[0.5\times10 + 0.5\times0\bigr] = \frac{5}{1.05}\approx \boxed{\$4.76}\]

**(c) Replicating portfolio ($\Delta$ shares $+$ bond $B$).**

Require $\Delta S_{u} + 1.05B = C_{u}$ and $\Delta S_{d} + 1.05B = C_{d}$: \[65\Delta + 1.05B = 10,\qquad 40\Delta + 1.05B = 0\] Subtracting: $25\Delta = 10 \Rightarrow \Delta = \dfrac{10}{25}= 0.40$ shares.

From the down equation: $1.05B = -16 \Rightarrow B = -\dfrac{16}{1.05}\approx -\$15.24$.

Portfolio value today: \[V_{0} = 0.4\times50 - \frac{16}{1.05}= 20 - 15.238 = \boxed{\$4.76}\] ✓\ matches part (b) by no-arbitrage.

Verify payoffs: \begin{itemize}\item Up: $0.4\times65 + 1.05\times(-16/1.05) = 26 - 16 = \$10$ ✓

\item Down: $0.4\times40 + 1.05\times(-16/1.05) = 16 - 16 = \$0$ ✓\end{itemize}

**Key takeaway:** Risk-neutral pricing and replicating portfolio always give the same answer by no-arbitrage. $\Delta=0.40$ means you hold 40\% of a share to hedge 1 call.

blocks.content:
**Two-Step Binomial Tree.** $S_{0}=\$50$, $u=1.2$, $d=0.85$, $r=3\%$ per period. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item Draw the price tree for two periods.

\item Find risk-neutral probability $p^{*}$.

\item Price a European put with $K=\$52$.

\item Price an American put with $K=\$52$. Does early exercise ever make sense?\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) Price tree.**
$t=0$$t=1$$t=2$
$\$60.00$$\nearrow$$\$72.00$
$\$50.00$$\nearrow$$\searrow$
$\searrow$$\nearrow$$\$51.00$
$\$42.50$$\searrow$
$\$36.12$
$S_{uu}=50\times1.44=72.00$,\; $S_{ud}=50\times1.02=51.00$,\; $S_{dd}=50\times0.7225=36.12$. **(b) Risk-neutral probability.** \[p^{*} = \frac{(1+r) - d}{u - d}= \frac{1.03 - 0.85}{1.2 - 0.85}= \frac{0.18}{0.35}= \boxed{\frac{18}{35}\approx0.5143}\] **(c) European put ($K=\$52$).** Terminal payoffs: $P_{uu}=0$,\; $P_{ud}=1$,\; $P_{dd}=52-36.125=15.875$. Period-1 values (discount by $1/(1+r)=1/1.03$): \[P_{u} = \frac{1}{1.03}\!\left(\tfrac{18}{35}\times0 + \tfrac{17}{35}\times1\right) = \frac{17}{35\times1.03}\approx \$0.4716\] \[P_{d} = \frac{1}{1.03}\!\left(\tfrac{18}{35}\times1 + \tfrac{17}{35}\times15.875\right) = \frac{8.225}{1.03}\approx \$7.985\] Period-0: \[P_{0} = \frac{1}{1.03}\!\left(\tfrac{18}{35}\times0.4716 + \tfrac{17}{35}\times7.985\right) \approx \frac{4.121}{1.03}\approx \boxed{\$4.00}\] **(d) American put.** At each node, take $\max(\text{intrinsic value},\;\text{continuation value})$. *Up node ($S_{u*=60$):} Intrinsic $=\max(52-60,0)=0$. Continuation $=0.4716 > 0$. Do not exercise: $P_{u}^{\text{Am}}=0.4716$. *Down node ($S_{d*=42.50$):} Intrinsic $=52-42.5=9.50$. Continuation $=7.985 < 9.50$. **Early exercise is optimal**: $P_{d}^{\text{Am}}=9.50$. *Time 0:* Intrinsic $=52-50=2$. Continuation value: \[P_{0}^{\text{cont}}= \frac{1}{1.03}\!\left(\tfrac{18}{35}\times0.4716 + \tfrac{17}{35}\times9.50\right) \approx \frac{4.857}{1.03}\approx 4.715\] Since $4.715 > 2$, do not exercise at time 0. \[\boxed{P_0^{\text{Am}} \approx \$4.72}\] The American put is worth more than the European put (\$4.72 vs \$4.00) because early exercise at the down node is optimal — intrinsic value \$9.50 exceeds the continuation value \$7.99. **Key takeaway:** American puts can be worth more than European puts due to early exercise. Early exercise is rational when the intrinsic value exceeds the continuation (time) value — typically for deep in-the-money puts when interest rates are positive.

blocks.content:
**Delta Hedging Intuition.** You sold 1 call with $\Delta=0.6$ on a stock at \$100. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item How many shares to hold for Delta-neutrality?

\item If stock rises \$1, how does the hedged portfolio change?

\item What does $\Gamma=0.05$ tell you?

\item Explain the Gamma–Theta trade-off for a long option.\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) Delta-neutral hedge.**

You are short 1 call with $\Delta=0.6$. Buy $\Delta=0.6$ shares of stock. Your portfolio: $+0.6$ shares $-1$ call.

Portfolio Delta: $0.6\times1 - 0.6 = 0$. Delta-neutral. ✓

**(b) Portfolio change for $\Delta S = +\$1$.**

Using the Delta approximation: \begin{itemize}\item Short call changes by $\approx -\Delta\cdot\Delta S = -0.6\times1 = -\$0.60$.

\item Long 0.6 shares change by $+0.6\times1 = +\$0.60$.\end{itemize} Net change: \[\Delta V_{\text{portfolio}}\approx -0.60 + 0.60 = \boxed{\$0}\] This is *exactly* what Delta-neutrality means: to first order, small stock price moves do not change the portfolio value.

**Note on second-order effects:** For large moves, the Gamma of the short call ($-0.05$) causes a *second-order* loss of $\frac{1}{2}\times0.05\times(\Delta S)^{2}$. But for a \$1 move, this is only $\approx\$0.025$ — small relative to the first-order effect that exactly cancels.

**(c) Gamma $=0.05$.**

Gamma measures the rate of change of Delta with respect to the stock price: \[\Gamma = \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial S^{2}}= 0.05\] If the stock rises by \$1, the call's Delta increases by approximately $0.05$: from 0.60 to 0.65. You must *rebalance* by buying $0.05$ more shares to remain Delta-neutral.

**(d) Gamma–Theta trade-off.**

For a **long option** position: \begin{itemize}\item **Gamma** $>0$: you benefit from large stock moves in either direction (convexity). A big move up or down increases your P&L.

\item **Theta** $<0$: you lose value as time passes (time decay). Every day you hold the option, it decays.\end{itemize}

The Black-Scholes PDE enforces: \[\Theta + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2} S^{2} \Gamma + rS\Delta = rV\] For an at-the-money option: $\Theta \approx -\frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2} S^{2}\Gamma$. A long position *pays* Theta (time decay) in exchange for *collecting* Gamma (profit from realised moves). If realised volatility exceeds implied volatility, the Gamma gains exceed the Theta cost: the position profits.

**Key takeaway:** Delta-neutral: hold $\Delta$ shares per short call. Gamma measures how fast Delta changes — large Gamma means frequent rebalancing needed. Long options: pay Theta, receive Gamma.

blocks.content:
**Black-Scholes: Assumptions, PDE, and Greeks.** \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item State the five key assumptions.

\item Write the Black-Scholes PDE.

\item For a long European call: sign and intuition for each Greek.

\item Behaviour of Greeks deep ITM vs deep OTM.\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) Black-Scholes assumptions.** \begin{enumerate}\item Stock price follows geometric Brownian motion: $dS = \mu S\,dt + \sigma S\,dW_{t}$. \item Constant volatility $\sigma$ and risk-free rate $r$. \item No dividends during the option's life. \item No transaction costs or taxes; continuous trading possible. \item No arbitrage opportunities.\end{enumerate} **(b) Black-Scholes PDE.** \[\frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2} S^{2}\,\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial S^{2}}+ rS\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial S}+ \frac{\partial C}{\partial t}= rC\] **(c) Greeks for a long European call.**
GreekSignIntuition
$\Delta = \partial C/\partial S$$+$ (0 to 1)Call value rises when stock rises.
$\Gamma = \partial^{2} C/\partial S^{2}$$+$Delta rises when stock rises (convexity). Call is always convex in $S$.
$\Theta = \partial C/\partial t$$-$Time decay: option loses value as expiry approaches (all else equal).
$\mathcal{V}= \partial C/\partial \sigma$ (Vega)$+$Higher volatility increases the probability of large favourable moves; call is worth more.
$\rho = \partial C/\partial r$$+$Higher rates reduce the PV of the strike payment; the call is worth more.
**(d) Deep ITM vs deep OTM.**
GreekDeep ITM ($S\gg K$)Deep OTM ($S\ll K$)
$\Delta$$\approx 1$ (acts like stock)$\approx 0$ (insensitive)
$\Gamma$$\approx 0$$\approx 0$
$\Gamma$ (ATM)\multicolumn{2}{c}{maximised at-the-money}
$\Theta$large negativesmall negative
Vega$\approx 0$$\approx 0$
Vega (ATM)\multicolumn{2}{c}{maximised at-the-money}
Deep ITM calls behave like stock ($\Delta\to1$). Deep OTM calls are nearly worthless and insensitive to small moves. Gamma and Vega are both maximised at-the-money where uncertainty about expiry outcome is highest. **Key takeaway:** BS PDE: $\frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2}S^{2}\Gamma + rS\Delta + \Theta = rC$. Call Greeks: $\Delta>0$, $\Gamma>0$, $\Theta<0$, Vega$>0$, $\rho>0$. Gamma and Vega peak ATM.

**(a) Volatility smile/smirk.**

In the Black-Scholes model, all options on the same underlying with the same expiry should have the same implied volatility $\sigma$ (since $\sigma$ is a constant input). In practice, if you back out the implied volatility from market prices, it varies with strike $K$: \begin{itemize}\item **Volatility smile:** Implied vol is higher for both low and high strikes (U-shaped vs ATM). Common in FX markets.

\item **Volatility skew/smirk:** Implied vol is monotonically higher for lower strikes. Typical in equity index markets.\end{itemize} This violates the BS assumption of constant $\sigma$, revealing that the model misprices options at extreme strikes.

**(b) Equity index skew: tail risk.**

For equity indices (e.g.\ S&P 500), deep out-of-the-money puts (low strike) trade at *higher* implied volatility than ATM options. This reflects: \begin{itemize}\item **Demand for crash protection:** Portfolio managers buy OTM puts to hedge against large market declines, bidding up their prices (and implied vol).

\item **Fat left tail / negative skewness:** Markets have historically crashed (fallen sharply) more often than they have “crashed upward”. The risk-neutral distribution implied by option prices has a fatter left tail than the lognormal assumed by Black-Scholes.\end{itemize}

**(c) Model extensions.** \begin{enumerate}\item **Stochastic volatility** (e.g.\ Heston 1993): $\sigma$ itself follows a mean-reverting process. Captures the smile because volatility clustering generates fat tails. The Heston model has a closed-form solution for European options.

\item **Local volatility** (Dupire 1994 / Derman-Kani): $\sigma = \sigma(S,t)$ is a deterministic function of stock price and time, calibrated to match all market prices exactly. Captures the smile by construction, but has poor dynamics for forward volatility.

\item **Jump-diffusion** (Merton 1976): adds Poisson jumps to the GBM. Sudden large drops generate a left tail fatter than lognormal, producing a skew.\end{enumerate}

**Key takeaway:** Volatility smile $=$ implied vol varies with strike, violating BS. Equity skew: OTM puts expensive due to crash demand and fat left tail. Extensions: stochastic vol (Heston), local vol (Dupire), jump-diffusion (Merton).

blocks.content:
**Topic 27 Technique Toolkit**
Put-Call Parity$C - P = S - Ke^{-rT}$. Violation $\Rightarrow$ sell overpriced side, buy underpriced plus hedge.
Risk-neutral prob.$p^{*} = (e^{rT}-d)/(u-d)$. Discount at $r$, not $\mu$.
Replicating portfolio$\Delta = (V_{u}-V_{d})/(S_{u}-S_{d})$; bond $B$ locks in value.
American vs European putAmerican $\geq$ European. Early exercise: intrinsic $>$ continuation value (deep ITM, high $r$).
Delta hedgeHold $\Delta$ shares per short call. Rebalance as $\Delta$ drifts (Gamma effect).
Gamma–ThetaLong option: $\Gamma>0$ (convexity gain), $\Theta<0$ (time decay). $\Theta \approx -\frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2} S^{2} \Gamma$ (ATM).
BS Greeks (call)$\Delta>0$, $\Gamma>0$, $\Theta<0$, Vega$>0$, $\rho>0$. $\Gamma$ and Vega peak ATM.
Vol smileEquity skew: OTM puts expensive. Models: stochastic vol (Heston), local vol (Dupire), jump-diffusion.
